---
title: "Get Started"
toc: true
---

This workshop runs entirely in the cloud using **GitHub Codespaces**. There is
nothing to install on your laptop — you only need a web browser and a free
GitHub account.

Step through the slides one at a time, or scroll down to read the full
instructions linearly.

## Instructions in slide form (long form below)
[Make slides full screen](getting-started-slides.qmd)

```{=html}
<iframe class="slide-deck" src="getting-started-slides.html" height="420" width="747" style="border: 1px solid #2e3846;"></iframe>
```

The order matters: create your repo, then add the API-key secret so it can
target that repo, **then** launch the codespace. A codespace launched before
the secret is in place won't see the key until you restart it.

## 1. Create your own copy of the workshop environment

The workshop environment lives in a **template** repository. You'll start by
making your own copy of it.

### Open the template

Open the [participant environment repo](https://github.com/elsherbini/data-science-for-biology-june2026-codespace)
on GitHub and click the green **"Use this template"** button near the top
right.

![](assets/images/getting-started/1-use-this-template.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="GitHub repo page with the 'Use this template' button highlighted in the top right"}

### Create a new repository

In the dropdown, choose **"Create a new repository"**.

![](assets/images/getting-started/2-create-new-repository.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="Use this template dropdown with 'Create a new repository' highlighted"}

### Name it

Give the repository a name (for example, `workshop-2026`) and click the green
**"Create repository"** button at the bottom of the form. The owner can stay
as your username.

![](assets/images/getting-started/3-give-repo-name.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="New repository form with the Repository name field highlighted"}

## 2. Add your OpenRouter API key

The workshop uses AI models through OpenRouter. Store your OpenRouter API
key as a Codespaces secret **before** you launch your codespace — that way
the codespace has the key from the moment it boots.

### Open your Codespaces settings

In the top-right of GitHub, click your profile photo and then **"Settings"**.
In the left sidebar of the settings page, navigate to **Codespaces** (or go
directly to <https://github.com/settings/codespaces>).

![](assets/images/getting-started/4-open-settings.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="GitHub profile dropdown with Settings highlighted"}

### Click "New secret"

Scroll to **"Codespace user secrets"** and click **"New secret"**.

![](assets/images/getting-started/5-add-new-secret.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="Codespace user secrets section with the New secret button highlighted"}

### Fill in and save the secret

In the form:

- **Name**: `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` (exact spelling, all caps)
- **Value**: paste the API key you were given for the workshop
- **Repository access**: click **"Select repositories"** and choose the
  repository you created in step 1

Then click **"Add secret"** at the bottom.

![](assets/images/getting-started/6-name-key-and-add-to-repo.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="New secret form with the Name field, Repository access dropdown, and Add secret button highlighted"}

## 3. Launch a codespace

Go back to your new repository's page. Click the green **"Code"** button,
switch to the **"Codespaces"** tab, and click **"Create codespace on main"**.

![](assets/images/getting-started/7-launch-codespace.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="Repository page with the Code button and Create codespace on main button highlighted in the Codespaces tab"}

The first build takes **around 10 minutes or more** — GitHub is provisioning
a container and installing R, Quarto, the workshop's R packages, and the Pi
coding agent. After this first build, reopening the codespace is fast.

## 4. Open a notebook

Once the codespace has finished building:

1. Use the file explorer on the left to find the starter Quarto notebooks
   (files ending in `.qmd`).
2. Open one to start working. You can edit text and code cells directly in
   the browser.

## 5. Meet Pi in the terminal

Your codespace includes **Pi**, an AI coding assistant, in the integrated
terminal. We will use Pi for the first couple of days; later in the workshop
we will switch to Claude Code.

1. Open the integrated terminal (**Terminal → New Terminal**, or use the
   keyboard shortcut).
2. Start Pi by typing `pi` and pressing Enter.
3. Ask it questions in plain language — it can read your notebook, explain
   code, and help you make changes.

![](assets/images/getting-started/8-meet-pi.png){.img-fluid fig-alt="Codespace with the integrated terminal open and the pi command typed at the prompt"}

You are now ready to begin the workshop. If you get stuck, ask an instructor
or a teaching assistant.
